title: About Me
body: I’m Han-yu Wang. You can call me Henry. I was born in Taiwan , grew up in Shanghai , am studying in Hong Kong , and have also spent meaningful stretches of time in Melbourne and around Hastings . I’m a fourth-year student majoring in Applied Artificial Intelligence at the University of Hong Kong . A lot of my academic work is about learning. I look at how people learn from experience, how AI systems pick up patterns, and why both can look sensible from the outside while something more complicated is happening underneath. For the academic and professional record in one place, visit my CV → 多良 · 太麻里 · 台東 Duoliang · Taimali · Taitung I also like building things around ideas. Some are practical tools, some are interactive explanations, and some are small systems that make scattered material easier to search, ask about, or play with. Recently, through Cloud, Data & AI consulting work at PwC, I have been thinking more about how AI systems can be built, evaluated, explained, and made useful inside real business workflows. The Projects page collects the fuller record, from company and lab work to coursework, side projects, and a few older things from secondary school. This website has slowly become part of the same habit, working as a portfolio, a personal archive, and a small playground. Use the , or press / , to ask about anything on the site.

title: On a More Personal Level
body: I like traveling, but not only as collecting places. What stays with me is usually more specific, like a ferry crossing Sydney Harbour at sunset, a seaside road in Taitung, the Acropolis in the heat, the Mediterranean water, or ordinary walks near the sea after a dense day. Melbourne mattered differently too, because it was not just an exchange semester. It became part of my family life and daily rhythm for a while. Click the compass to explore places that have stayed with me. When I’m not working, you’ll often find me by the water. In New Zealand it was Napier’s oceanside. In Hong Kong it is often Kennedy Town Praya. Elsewhere it may just be a ferry, a harbour, or a long walk along the coast. The sea resets me somehow. For the less formal side, Fragments collects some of my looser thoughts and small life pieces, from AI, memory, and language to travel, family, food, and ordinary moments that stayed with me.

title: Research I've Been Part Of
body: My research asks how representations are formed, changed, used, and controlled across minds and machines. I am interested in how experience becomes structure, how that structure supports memory, reasoning, generalization, and action, and why it sometimes becomes unstable, misleading, or difficult to control. On the human side, I study learning as a problem of representation. How do people move from examples to rules, from local regularities to reusable structure, and from partial evidence to a sense of what matters? I use behavior and models to ask what kind of structure a person may have built, how that structure changes with experience, and how it guides later judgment. Some of this work is done as a part-time research assistant at HKU's SLR Lab (https://slrlab.edu.hku.hk/), with Dr. Stephen M. K. Lee. On the AI side, I study model interpretability, transparency, and the mechanisms behind model behavior. I want to understand not only whether a model gives the right answer, but what internal structures and computations make that answer possible, where they fail, and how much of the process can be made inspectable enough to reason about. The goal is a more precise account of what models represent, how those representations are used, and when an apparent capability has a stable mechanism behind it. I am also interested in the broader relationship between people and AI systems. As these systems become part of learning, writing, work, and decision-making, they change what people attend to, what they trust, what they practice, and what they stop doing themselves. I want to study those changes at the level of cognition and design: how ability is distributed between person and tool, how control and understanding can be preserved, and how AI can help people think better rather than simply move faster. The long-term aim is to let the study of minds and models inform one another. Work on human learning can make AI systems more interpretable and more useful; work on AI can gi

title: Insurance Product Configuration Engine
body: An engine that turns an insurance product into the exact app screens it needs Associated with HSBC (https://www.hsbc.com/) On HSBC's insurance digital-transformation team, I tackled a quietly painful problem: across dozens of insurance products and a dozen markets, which app screens should each product actually show? Those rules lived scattered across business, design, and engineering, and kept drifting out of sync. I encoded them into one structure: three linked libraries plus a decision table, driven by an Excel and VBA engine that turns a product's specification into the exact set of UI components it needs, automatically. It went through four versions and shipped with a built-in tutorial so the team could keep running it after I left. Because the real logic is dense, I rebuilt a small, playable version you can drive in your browser, with a walkthrough that explains the problem before it hands you the controls: Try the interactive walkthrough → July 2024 - April 2025

title: Interactive Scrollytelling Deck
body: A Schema-Driven Scrollytelling Engine with a Pluggable D3 Chart Library This started as a course project: I had a data-heavy topic to present and wanted to tell it as a story that unfolds while you scroll, not a wall of charts. It grew well past the assignment into a reusable scrollytelling engine. Every scene is declared in a single schema, so text, tables, cards, and charts share consistent layout and reveal timing, with a registry mapping each figure to a D3 chart builder: line, ridge, scatter, timeline, heatmap, geo maps, Sankey, and chord. A background manager streams a different looping video per section, and it all runs as plain ES modules with no build step, so it hosts anywhere static. The live demo below is the course deck itself, built entirely on the engine. title: Stride Support
body: Customer Support Copilot with MCP Tools and Local LLM Stride Support is a customer support copilot that runs entirely on-device. A FastAPI router speaks the OpenAI-compatible API and orchestrates five MCP (Model Context Protocol) tool servers (catalog search, order lookups, complaints, recommendations, and policy QA) over a local LLM via LM Studio. The React UI lets the user pick from ongoing or past orders, and the router injects that context into every prompt so the model stays grounded in what the customer actually has. A semantic similarity gate blocks off-topic queries, and a fine-tuned mBERT classifier tags sentiment in real time so the model can adjust tone. Project page: github.com/backprop07/STAT4011 (https://github.com/backprop07/STAT4011) On my own time Side projects, built outside work and class. June 2026 - July 2026

title: How This Search Was Built
body: Search by Meaning and Grounded Q&A, Running Inside a Static Site This site can search itself by meaning and answer questions about me in full sentences, with no search server behind it. When the site is built, every passage is turned into a list of numbers that captures what it is about; in the browser, a typed question becomes the same kind of numbers and is matched against them, so searching by the idea can still turn up a project like Lexaday even when the page never uses the words you typed. Asking a question goes one step further: the whole site is handed to a small model running on free services, which replies in plain sentences and links back to the pages it used. The walkthrough page takes the lid off, with a star map of the site laid out by meaning, a side-by-side look at how the ranking is put together, and a cut-off line you can drag to see what still counts as a match. title: Lexaday
body: A Daily Vocabulary Coach with Generative Mnemonics and Spaced Review Lexaday is a small learning system that turns word roots into a daily email habit. Each run chooses a new root, generates a short motivational hook, creates example words and sentences, attaches a visual mnemonic, and brings back older roots on a growing review interval. The project keeps per-user progress in JSON, commits generated images and state through GitHub Actions, and uses Hugging Face models for both language and image generation so the email feels more like a personalized lesson than a static flashcard. title: Insurance Product Configuration Engine
body: Here's something you've done a hundred times: packing for a trip . The kind of trip decides what goes in the bag. Say "beach" and you need swimwear; add "water sports" and a snorkel earns its place; say "ski" and it's a completely different bag. You're running a tiny rules engine in your head, turning a few answers into the right list. At HSBC I built exactly that engine, for a much harder version of the same problem. Below is a small, fully playable version. It takes a couple of minutes, and the second half hands you the controls.

title: First, the real problem
body: Picture one banking app that sells many kinds of insurance. Every product needs a different set of screens : a travel policy asks for trip dates, a car policy asks about your vehicle, and some questions only appear once an earlier answer unlocks them. Now multiply that by dozens of products across a dozen markets. The rules for "which screen shows when" lived scattered in people's heads. Business knew the requirements, designers knew the screens, developers wired it together, and over time the three drifted out of sync. There was no single place you could point to and say this is how a product becomes a set of screens.

title: The idea: stop mixing three questions
body: I split the mess into three small libraries, each owned by the team that actually knows the answer: Spec What does the business need to know? The questions. Components What has the team actually built? The building blocks. Config Small rule tables linking the two: given these answers, use these blocks. Because they're separate, business can add a question, design can add a block, and neither breaks the other. Those rule tables are where the magic happens, and they're what you'll drive next.

title: Now you try it
body: Same machine, on the friendly example: you're packing for a trip. Answer the questions, watch each little rule table pick a winner, then watch the bag fill with exactly the right things. 1 Tell us about the trip ↳ Notice the follow-up question appears only when it's relevant. In the real tool that condition is written right into the field's name, so the form reshapes itself as you fill it in. 2 Each decision gets its own rule table Every table works the same way: keep the columns whose conditions all match your answers, and the most specific one on the right wins. ALL is a wildcard that matches anything. Your answers (inputs) What to pack (outputs) 1"> Your answers match columns; the most specific, “ ” , wins. Your answers land on “ ” . 3 The bag packs itself Packing list Just the essentials for this trip. Same answers in, the same list out, every time. Each table contributes its part, and together they build the bag. Nobody has to remember the rules, because the tables do.

title: What this really was
body: The real version ran in Excel, driven by a roughly 32 KB VBA engine. It had a small custom language for those conditional follow-up questions, a stack of decision tables with wildcards and right-side overrides (exactly what you just used), and live form behaviour so non-coders could fill it in without breaking anything. It went through four versions and shipped with a built-in tutorial so the next person could take it over. The packing example here stands in for confidential product data, but the machinery is the real thing: a way to turn scattered, tribal business logic into one structure that a team, and a computer, can both read. ← Back to projects

title: Lexaday
body: Lexaday rests on a few ideas I find convincing. Learning a word's root is leverage: one root carries a whole family of words, so a single lesson stretches far. Spacing the reviews along the forgetting curve means a word resurfaces just as it starts to fade, which is when the reminder actually does something. And generative AI does the legwork, fleshing out each root with example sentences and a mnemonic image instead of a bare definition. It all comes as one small email a day.

title: A small lesson, generated fresh each morning
body: Instead of a static flashcard, Lexaday builds a whole email around one root: what it means, the real words it shows up in, a sentence for each, and an image to anchor the meaning.

title: The learning loop
body: It comes down to roots and spacing. Learn "aud" (to hear) and audible, audition and auditorium come almost for free. And rather than drilling a root every day, Lexaday widens the gap each time it returns, one run, then two, four, eight, so a review lands just as the word starts to fade. Everyone has their own progress file, so the timing fits how much they have already seen. 1

title: Pick
body: Choose one new root the user has not seen yet, while scanning past roots for scheduled review. 2

title: Write
body: Generate a short motivational hook and clear example sentences for the words built from that root. 3

title: Imagine
body: Turn the root meaning into a visual prompt, then create a mnemonic image for the email. 4

title: Send
body: Build an HTML email, attach the generated images inline, save the new state, and repeat tomorrow.

title: What the reader receives
body: The email is plain on purpose: open Gmail, read one root, look at the picture, skim a few examples, and revisit a couple of older roots. The work that produced it stays out of sight, so the daily habit costs little. A generated email pairs the root with its meaning, a visual mnemonic, example words, and a review section when older roots are due.

title: How it is packaged
body: Personal memory Each user has a lightweight JSON record for run count, first introduction, last review, and review interval. Generated content Language generation fills in quotes and examples; image generation produces mnemonic artwork for the root. Daily automation A scheduled GitHub Actions run refreshes lessons, images, and state without needing a separate app interface. Project repo: github.com/henryhyw/lexaday (https://github.com/henryhyw/lexaday) ← Back to projects

title: Dealtracker
body: I spent a semester on exchange in Melbourne, Australia, and the outdoor gear I'd wanted back home, Arc'teryx, Patagonia and the like, turned out to be both cheaper than in Hong Kong or the mainland and almost always discounted somewhere. The catch was timing: a sale would surface and vanish, lost in pages and pages of products. Dealtracker is what I built so I'd stop missing them. It watches the shops for me and emails me the moment something I actually want drops to a price I'd actually pay.

title: From noisy sale pages to a short inbox digest
body: The idea is simple: pick the stores, spell out exactly what I want, brand, size, gender, minimum discount, then let a scheduled job read the sale pages for me and surface only the handful of matches worth opening. 2 Australian stores 7 brands I track 29-39% minimum discount

title: The email output
body: The output is the email itself: matching products grouped by brand and sorted by discount, each with a photo, the original and sale price, the percentage off, and a link straight to the product. Arc'teryx and The North Face digest Patagonia and Icebreaker digest

title: What happens behind the email
body: It does one narrow job: check these shops on a schedule, and email me only when something I'd actually buy has gone on sale. To do that it scrapes two Australian outdoor retailers, WildEarth and Find Your Feet. Search Build store-specific sale URLs from saved filters for brands, sizes, gender, and minimum discount. Read Open the pages in a headless browser, including dynamic product grids that need time to load. Clean Normalize brand names such as Arc'teryx versus Arcteryx and detect product gender from titles. Compare Check each result against a saved history file so the email contains new deals, not repeated noise. Notify Send a compact HTML digest only when something new passes the filters.

title: The control layer
body: Dealtracker also keeps half an eye on the inbox. Emailing it a one-word reset clears the saved history, which is handy when I want to start the baseline fresh without touching the script or the data file. Specific filters Each store has its own URL pattern, so filters are encoded in the way that store actually expects. Human-friendly output The email is built to be acted on: brand sections, product cards, prices, and links. Low-maintenance memory A local history file keeps past product links and names, preventing the same sale from being announced repeatedly. Project repo: github.com/henryhyw/dealtracker (https://github.com/henryhyw/dealtracker) ← Back to projects

title: Scrollytelling Engine
body: A course left me with a data-heavy topic to present: maps, distributions, and flows that only land once you can actually see them. Static slides could not carry it. I wanted the data to animate, and the argument to unfold as you read rather than all at once, so I built it as a web page instead, a story you move through by scrolling. Partway in, the machinery turned out to be general, so I pulled it into a small engine I can point at any subject. The deck at the bottom is one thing made with it.

title: A scene is just data
body: Here is the idea, running live. Every scene below is drawn by the engine's own code, the same builders the real deck uses. You describe a figure by its type and a few props, a registry hands it to the matching builder, and it animates itself in. Step through them. Add a scene type and it is one entry in the registry; use it and it is a few lines of data. You never touch render code.

title: See it as a story
body: The pieces above are the vocabulary. Below is the deck they were built for, running in full and embedded right here. Scroll inside it: the visuals hold while the words and data move past, which is the whole point. Cramped in the frame? Open it fullscreen ↗ (https://www.henryw.me/CCGL9074/)

title: Under the hood
body: Declarative slides Each scene is one object: its background group, its nav label, and the figures it holds. Layout, typography, and reveal timing come from shared tokens. A figure registry The type on a figure is looked up in a registry of builders, covering text, line, ridge, scatter, heatmap, geo maps, Sankey, chord, and more. Background manager Looping video layers stream per section group, so each part of the story carries its own mood without any extra markup. Zero build Plain ES modules with D3, d3-sankey, and TopoJSON loaded over CDN. It runs from any static host with nothing to compile. Live demo: henryw.me/CCGL9074 (https://www.henryw.me/CCGL9074/) Project repo: github.com/henryhyw/CCGL9074 (https://github.com/henryhyw/CCGL9074) ← Back to projects

title: My Early Adventures in Computer Science
body: Here are the adventures I went through during my junior high and high school years. I've highlighted some main projects from each year. I'll admit upfront: a lot of what I did back then was pretty naive. But who hasn't had a naive phase, right? Honestly, I might still be a bit naive now, and that's okay.

title: How It All Started
body: I fell in love with computer science when I was 13, in my first year of junior high school. My computer science teacher demonstrated how a batch script can create folders automatically. From that moment, I was fascinated by how computers could be programmed to do all sorts of cool things. I taught myself the basics of Java and Python and started building simple apps, like a contact notebook app in Java and an advanced number guessing game with user accounts and game history logged in text files. Here are some screenshots of this first app (contact notebook) I made.

title: Batch Downloading Scientific American Episodes
body: In my first year of junior high, I decided to practice English and discovered the podcast called Scientific American. At that time, I was using an MP3 player that supported playing audio, images, and texts. So, I developed an app to batch download audios, scripts, and images from Scientific American episodes. The app included a user account system that could record the episodes a user had downloaded and continue from where they left off next time. Here are some screenshots of this app.

title: Chrome Extension Experiments
body: In my second year of junior high, I started exploring Chrome extensions. Living in Mainland China made me curious about things that weren't easily accessible, so I'd use a VPN to access Google-related stuff. It amazed me that these small apps in the browser could do so much, like turning the screen dark for YouTube videos. So, I learned how to develop and publish Chrome extensions and created a few: Easy QR Code : Displays QR codes for the current website, makes your own QR codes, and converts text to QR codes. Page Muter : Mutes the audio or video of a specific tab. Image Fetcher : Batch downloads images from the current website. SA Fetcher : Batch downloads audios, scripts, and images from Scientific American episodes. This is a simplified version of the previous SA Fetcher app. WeChat Spamming : Automatically sends various kinds of spam messages on WeChat (I made this to prank my friends, and surprisingly, it even has 167 users according to the developer dashboard). Here are some screenshots of the developer dashboard and some of the extensions.

title: Music Streaming Hacks
body: In my first year of high school, my main interest was building an app for downloading paid songs from music streaming services in China. I now realize that this was illegal, but at the time, I wasn't aware of that. Although I'm not proud of it, I still want to mention it because it was a masterpiece for a first-year high school student. I did a lot of research on web scraping and managed to crack the links of paid songs from various music streaming services. With this app, users could download songs with the link of the song page from QQ Music, Netease Music, KuGou Music, and KuWo Music, with the desired sound quality. Users could also batch download songs from a singer with a specified number of pages. I even created a user account system where some functions, like downloading lossless audio, were only available to VIP users. It took me several months to develop this app, but it only lasted for half a year because I later realized it wasn't quite legal, so I shut it down. Despite that, it remains one of my fondest memories. By that time, it had 413 users, with 228 QQ Music playlist downloads, 1294 QQ Music single downloads, 523 NetEase Music single downloads, 23 KuGou Music single downloads, and 47 KuWo Music single downloads. Here are some screenshots of the app, which is in Simplified Chinese since the users were from Mainland China.

title: Building Kiwiview's Website
body: In my second year of high school, I started building a website for Kiwiview International Limited, a New Zealand company co-owned by my parents and a Kiwi. Since the business was just starting, they let me try building the website. I taught myself HTML, JavaScript, CSS, and SQL. It took me months to deploy it on an IIS server on Tencent Cloud. The deployment process was really complicated for a beginner, and I spent a lot of time researching it.

title: Simple Automation
body: In my third year of high school, I came across a mental arithmetic and "24-point" game event. It was an online game event held at our school. I created a script to automatically solve the problems, achieving really high scores. The script recognized the content on the screen, used algorithms to solve the problems, and then instructed the computer to input the answers. Everything was done automatically and repeatedly until time was up. Here are some videos of the script I recorded. Your browser does not support the video tag. Your browser does not support the video tag.

title: Econographer, an Economics Graphs Generator
body: In my third year of high school, I started to wonder if the graphs we needed for our economics class could be generated by software. After searching online and not finding anything that met my expectations, I decided to create my own solution. I developed an Office Word add-in called "Econographer," specifically designed for generating the various types of economics graphs used in AP and IB courses. These graphs are highly customizable to fit any requirement. Here's a full demonstration video, recorded by me, showing how it works. Your browser does not support the video tag. I hope you enjoyed reading about my early journey with computer science! ← Back to projects

title: How This Search Was Built
body: This is a static site with no search server behind it, yet it can be searched by meaning and can answer questions in full sentences. The whole site is turned into vectors when it is built, the browser only handles the query, and a small edge worker writes the answers. Everything below is wired to the real index and the real model, so it can be tried directly rather than just described. The site laid out by meaning

title: A map of everything written here
body: Each passage of the site is turned into a 384-dimensional vector, and UMAP flattens those vectors onto a plane so that meaning becomes position. Research passages settle in one region, travel notes in another, and the fragments sit between them. A typed question is embedded the same way and dropped onto the map, landing next to the passages it is closest to. The nearest-passage list here is semantic only: it shows the closest neighbours in vector space and nothing more. The search used on the rest of the site does more than that, and the panel below takes it apart.

title: Two rankers, one query
body: Every query is read by two different rankers. A word-based ranker scores passages by rare shared words, so it is precise when a name is typed in full, such as "HSBC" or "Lexaday", and lost when the same idea is only paraphrased. A meaning-based ranker compares vectors instead, so it can follow a paraphrase about memorising vocabulary back to Lexaday, but it drifts on short literal names. Since neither is reliable on its own, the two rankings are merged (a weighted reciprocal rank fusion), with the strongest word matches held near the top so an exact hit is never buried. The columns below show where the two agree and where they part. Lexical rare shared words No word overlap at all. Semantic cosine similarity Fused what the palette shows

title: Drawing the line
body: Search has a problem that Ask does not: it has to know when to say "no matches". Every query is scored against all passages, and a floor has to separate a weak match from an unrelated one. The floor was tuned on a set of real queries and clearly unrelated ones: the unrelated queries stayed at or below about 0.22, genuine matches mostly sat above 0.45, and a few real matches dipped to about 0.26. The line was set at 0.24 to lean towards recall. That wide gap is also why this model was kept: e5-small was tried and it squeezed every score into a narrow band where no fixed floor worked, without improving recall enough to be worth it. Run a query above, then drag the line to see what survives. = floor }" :style="{ left: scoreX(s) + '%' }" > Run a query in the lab above and the score distribution appears here.

title: From search to answers
body: Ask adds a writer on top of the same retrieval. The site is small enough that all of it can be handed to the model at once, so an answer is never lost to an unlucky retrieval, and the ranking above is used mainly to choose which fragment entries come along. A Cloudflare Worker at the edge writes the answer, falling back through a chain of free-tier models, while a twin function on EdgeOne serves visitors in mainland China. The model is told to use only the passages it was given and to say plainly when the site does not cover something. 1

title: Gather
body: The whole site is handed over at once instead of a lucky top-k, and the ranking chooses which fragment entries join it. 2

title: Prompt
body: The passages are numbered into a grounded prompt that rules out invention and asks for citations. 3

title: Generate
body: An edge worker writes the answer on free-tier models, falling back down the chain if one is unavailable. 4

title: Cite
body: Every fact carries a bracketed number back to its page, and only the sources actually used are shown.

title: The constraints that shaped it
body: No search backend The corpus is embedded once at build time and shipped as int8 vectors. Searching is a dot product in your browser; typing here sends nothing anywhere. One model, both sides Build script and browser load the exact same MiniLM checkpoint, so document vectors and query vectors share one space. Mixing checkpoints quietly breaks cosine. Answers on the edge Generation is the only part that needs a server, and it is a single worker with a fallback chain of free-tier models, plus a mirror on a second edge network for reachability. Free end to end Embeddings cost nothing after build, retrieval costs nothing ever, and answering rides free tiers. The whole system runs on roughly zero dollars a month. All of it comes together in the palette itself: open search and ask something (or press / anywhere on the site). ← Back to projects

title: Fragments
body: Loose fragments of thought and life. Fragment entries are indexed from the shared fragment data source.

title: The Projection of Thought
body: When thought becomes language, it is not only shortened. A layered, moving state is projected onto a surface, and that surface can start guiding the thought in return.

title: The Projection of Thought (2)
body:
I used to think of expression mainly as compression. A thought is larger than a sentence, and to express it I have to cut it into words, put them in order, and leave some things out. That still feels right, but compression may not be enough to describe what is happening. It makes it sound as if a thought holds a fixed amount of content and expression simply removes part of it, keeping some details and losing others so the rest is easier to store and pass on. That is part of it, but the deeper change may be that expression projects the thought onto another surface.

Before it is expressed, a thought is not only a set of contents. It is also a movement, with pressure, direction, hesitation, unfinished relations, bodily feeling, memory, and some sense of where it might want to go next. Many of these do not stand apart; they exist together, pull on each other, and change each other. A thought can be non-linear in that sense. One example may not matter on its own but only because of how it sits beside another; one word may feel wrong not because it is false but because it would make the whole path turn too early; a vague feeling may hold several possible directions before any of them becomes clear.

When I put this into language, the movement has to become a line. One sentence comes first, then another; a relation becomes a phrase, a pressure becomes a claim, a hesitation becomes a qualification, a half-formed direction becomes a title or a category or a possible next step. This does not only remove things, it changes their relations. What was simultaneous becomes sequential, what was layered becomes flatter, what was still moving becomes a more stable object, what was not yet divided may be cut into parts, and what had several possible directions may begin to look like one path.

So expression is a projection. I do not mean this in a strict mathematical way, only that something with more layers and movement becomes visible on a smaller surface. The surface is real and i

title: The Projection of Thought (3)
body:
This is why a written thought can become both clearer and thinner: clearer because it has gained form, thinner because some relations that once lived behind it may no longer be active. Sometimes that is fine. A thought often has to become thinner to become usable, and if everything stays folded together, nothing can be said, written, communicated, or developed. But sometimes the flattening happens too early. A thought may still need its ambiguity, still need several directions to stay alive, still need to resist becoming one clean claim, and if I project it too quickly into a sentence or a title, the surface may decide the path before the thought has finished moving.

This is not only about losing content; it is also about losing the developmental process of the thought. A thought does not only have a current state, it also has a possible unfolding. It may be moving toward one problem and then turn because another memory becomes relevant; it may look like it is about language and later reveal that it is really about memory, self, or agency; it may need time to discover what kind of thought it is. Projection can shorten that. Once a thought is expressed in one form, later thinking tends to follow the form. If I call it a problem about language I may keep developing it as a language problem; if I call it a problem about memory I may start looking for memory-related examples. The title becomes a route, the route becomes easier to walk, and other routes become harder to notice. That is useful when the title is right, and limiting when the title is only the first surface the thought happened to take.

So the question is not only whether a projected thought is accurate, but whether it still lets the thought continue becoming what it was not yet able to become. A bad projection does not have to be false. It may be true at the surface and still harmful to the movement, capturing what I said but not what was still trying to form, preserving one relation while making another

title: The Projection of Thought (4)
body:
This is also where AI becomes interesting. AI works very well on projected surfaces. It can take my words, notes, fragments, and drafts and organize them into cleaner structures, name the direction, sharpen the distinction, arrange the argument, and show paths I had not seen, and that can be genuinely helpful. But because it works on the projected version, it may also intensify the projection. It may take a surface that was only temporary and make it look like the real structure, turn a fragile direction into a confident outline, turn a hesitation into a neat qualification, and turn several live possibilities into one elegant frame. The problem is not that AI necessarily gets things wrong; the more serious problem is that it can make one projected version too convincing. Then I may keep going from the AI-organized surface and forget to return to the wider movement, so the thought becomes more polished while the process by which it was still discovering itself is cut short.

So AI does not create the problem from nowhere. Expression already projects thought and writing already gives it a surface; AI simply enters after projection has happened and can organize the surface with unusual speed and force. That means the responsibility is not to reject AI organization but to keep moving between the surface and the deeper field. When AI gives me a structure, I need to ask whether it only organizes the visible text or whether it helps me recover the movement that produced it: does it preserve the pressure, keep the unresolved relation alive, let the thought continue, or does it make the thought look finished too early? If I can do that, AI may become useful in a deeper way, giving me a surface that helps me see the movement again. If I cannot, I may slowly start thinking only from projected surfaces, and the thought will look clearer while also becoming flatter.

Maybe this is why I need fragments to stay open. A fragment is a projection of a moment, not the final form of th

title: Rebuilding Another State Inside Me
body: Empathy may be less like directly receiving another feeling, and more like rebuilding another state inside my own system from the signals it leaves outside.

title: Rebuilding Another State Inside Me (2)
body:
I cannot actually enter another person's state. This sounds obvious, but it matters more than it first seems. Their real state is inside them, and their body, memory, history, habits, fear, shame, desire, and the way a certain event touches all of these are not directly available to me. What I can see is what comes out: words, facial expression, tone, silence, movement, a change in posture, a story they tell, something they avoid saying, the situation around them. These are outputs, traces from a system I cannot open directly.

So empathy cannot be simple access. It is more like reconstruction. Another person gives off signals, and my own system tries to build a state that corresponds to them; I take in the visible part and then use my own memory, body, experience, imagination, and learned sense of people to generate something inside me. That thing inside me is not exactly their feeling, but it is not just my ordinary feeling either. It is a state produced in me, but pulled by them.

This is where empathy starts to become interesting to me. It is not only about whether I know what someone feels, which is too shallow; the real question is what kind of state gets generated inside me once I have received their signals. At a shallow level I may only label it, he is sad, she is embarrassed, this person is anxious, this person feels left out. That recognition is useful, but it still feels external, as if I have classified the output while nothing much has happened inside my own system. At a deeper level, the other person's state starts changing mine. My attention moves toward them, my body may tighten a little, my voice may soften, I may become more careful with my words, or feel an urge to stop explaining, to comfort, to stay quiet, to protect, to move closer.

This is probably why I keep thinking empathy needs some kind of internal signal, not necessarily the same signal the other person has, which would be too strong, but some movement in my body, attention, emotion, o

title: Rebuilding Another State Inside Me (3)
body:
So empathy has a strange double movement: it brings me closer to another person, and it also rebuilds that person through me. This is why I do not think empathy is automatically pure or correct. A person can empathize deeply and still be wrong, and sometimes the deeper reconstruction is more dangerous, because I start trusting the feeling inside me too much. I feel something strongly, so I assume I have reached them, when maybe I have only reached a version of them my own system produced. This does not make empathy fake; it only means empathy is not a direct copy of another mind. It is a model, but not a cold one, a reconstruction, but not only a cognitive one, a state inside me that tries to correspond to a state outside me.

Then self-empathy becomes a special case. At first it sounds easier: if the state I am trying to feel is my own, maybe I do not need to reconstruct another system, since it is my own body history and memory line and past. But I do not think it is that simple. The past version of me is also not directly available to the present me, and a hidden part of me is not fully open either. A feeling I pushed away, a state I did not understand at the time, a pressure I lived through and later compressed into a story, none of these are just sitting there in their original form. They leave traces: a memory, a reaction, a kind of avoidance, a bodily discomfort, a preference I cannot explain, a sudden resistance to something, a sense that a certain old moment still matters even after I have explained it many times.

So self-empathy may also be reconstruction, where the present me takes traces from a past or hidden me and tries to rebuild that state inside the current system. This is not the same as self-understanding. I can explain myself very well and still not really be close to myself. I can say why I reacted that way, where the pattern came from, what experience shaped it, and turn it into a clean story, and yet the explanation can feel too far above the

title: Rebuilding Another State Inside Me (4)
body:
It is not exactly the internal state itself; it is another proxy, but a proxy different from language. It does not explain the state as quickly, and it lets the state sit there in a form I can look at before I know exactly what it means. That looking matters. The image came from me, but once it is outside it is no longer fully trapped inside me, so I can face it, move around it, notice what part pulls my attention, and feel whether it makes me tense, calm, sad, embarrassed, or unexpectedly familiar. In that moment the image may help rebuild a state that language could only summarize. Maybe this is why something like a mandala can feel connected to self-empathy, not because drawing a shape magically heals anything, but because it gives a hard-to-access internal state an external surface, so the state becomes something I can meet again.

So empathy, self-empathy, language, and drawing may all be different ways of dealing with states that are not directly accessible. With another person, I see outputs and rebuild a state inside me; with a past or hidden self, I see traces and rebuild a state inside the present me; with language, I compress the state into words; with drawing, I may let the state become visible before it is fully explained. None of these gives perfect access, but maybe perfect access is not the point. The point is whether a state that was outside my immediate reach can become active enough inside me to change how I feel, understand, or act. That state may belong to someone else, to a past version of me, or to a part of me that has not yet become clear. Either way, I am not simply receiving it. I am rebuilding it inside me, using the only system I have.

title: The Visible Part of the Mind
body: Consciousness may not be the whole mind becoming visible, but the part of the mind that becomes reachable through the lens of the current moment.

title: The Visible Part of the Mind (2)
body:
Sometimes I feel that what I consciously think is much smaller than what is actually moving inside my mind. A thought appears, and because it appears clearly enough, I may take it for the thought itself: I can hold it, say it, write it down, continue from it. But very often the thing that appears in consciousness is only a small visible part of something much larger. Before I know what I think, many things may already be moving, memory, mood, body, recent experience, language, old associations, unfinished thoughts, attention, the current situation, and most of them do not appear directly. I do not see them as separate causes; I only receive the result, which may be a sentence, a feeling, a sudden preference, a vague sense that something is wrong, or the feeling that I understand something before I can explain why.

So maybe consciousness is not where a thought begins, but where something becomes reachable. This distinction matters to me. Something can already be shaping me before I can consciously access it: a memory can affect my reaction without appearing as a memory, a mood can change how I read a situation without announcing itself, a past experience can make one idea feel natural and another feel wrong even when I cannot trace the path. When something finally becomes conscious, it is not necessarily being created in that moment; it may simply have become available to the part of me that can notice, hold, name, report, and keep working on it.

Even then, what becomes conscious is still not the whole structure. It is more like a projection, and I do not mean that in a precise mathematical sense, only that a larger internal state is being shown through a smaller surface. Something complex becomes visible as something simpler: a whole background of memory, emotion, body, and attention becomes one thought, one image, one sentence, one feeling. And the projection is not neutral. The same hidden material can become different conscious contents depending on how I am re

title: The Visible Part of the Mind (3)
body:
This may be why the same thought can change when I return to it later. I may think I am continuing the same idea when I am really reading it through a different state. The old material is still there, but the current lens has changed, new mood, new language, new context, new pressure, new experience, so the thought comes out differently. It also makes me think that language is not the first compression. I used to treat expression as the moment when a vague thought becomes compressed into language, which still feels right, but maybe before language compresses thought, consciousness has already compressed it once. A large hidden state becomes a conscious surface, then the surface becomes a sentence, then the sentence becomes a written note, then the note becomes something I later read and continue from, and at each step something is preserved and something is lost.

This is why I should not fully trust the conscious version either. It is closer to me than the written version, but it is still not the whole thing, already shaped by attention, access, mood, language, and the way I am reading myself. And yet I need it. Without the visible part I cannot work with anything, because I cannot hold the whole hidden structure directly; I need something to appear, a sentence, a feeling, a rough image, a small handle, and consciousness gives me that handle. But the handle is not the whole mind. Maybe that is the strange thing about consciousness: it feels like the place where I meet myself most directly, yet it may also be one of the first places where I have already been simplified. What I can report is not nothing, but it is not the whole thing. It is the visible part of the mind, made reachable through the lens of the current moment.

title: Before Defining Understanding
body: Maybe understanding should not be defined too quickly, because the word often gathers many different questions before we have separated them.

title: Before Defining Understanding (2)
body:
I do not want to define understanding too early, not because the word is meaningless, since it clearly points to something important, but because it gathers too many things before I have separated them. Sometimes understanding means being able to give the right answer, sometimes being able to explain, and sometimes it means transfer, generalization, correction, compression, use, experience, grounding, or consciousness. These are related, but they are not the same, so if I define understanding too quickly I may only be choosing one layer and pretending I have answered the whole question.

This matters for both people and machines. A student may solve a problem correctly without really knowing why; a person may explain something fluently while missing the structure underneath; a model may give a good answer without failing or adapting the way a person would. In all of these the word understanding is tempting, but also unstable. That is why I think the first step should not be to decide whether something understands, but to ask which part of understanding I am talking about. Am I asking whether it can answer, whether it can explain, whether it can use the idea in a new situation, whether it can notice when the surface changes but the structure stays the same, whether it can revise itself after being wrong, whether it has experience behind the behavior? These questions may all belong near understanding, but they should not be collapsed into one word too quickly.

The same problem shows up in the usual contrast between correlation and causation. People often say machines learn correlations while humans understand causes, and there is something right in that, but it is also too clean. Humans also begin with regularities; we see one thing appear with another and later expect them together, and that alone is not yet causation. But a regularity can become an assumption, the assumption can guide action, the world can push back, and the assumption can be corrected, refined, or

title: Expression, Recording, and the Compressed Version of Thought
body: Expression preserves a thought by turning it into language, but the same act also compresses and reshapes it.

title: Expression, Recording, and the Compressed Version of Thought (2)
body:
When a thought is still in my head, it is usually not a sentence. It is more like a mass of things: a feeling, a direction, some past experience, and connections I cannot quite explain even to myself. Most of the time I do not first have a complete sentence and then say it out loud; I have something vague but real, and language cuts it apart, puts it in order, and compresses it into a form other people can understand. That matters, because without language many thoughts would just stay inside my head. Once I say something, it becomes something I can work with. Other people can understand it and push back on it, I can look at it again, and it can be saved, remembered, organized further, and carried into the next round of thinking.

So expression is not a bad thing. Many thoughts only become usable after they are expressed; before that they are just a state in the mind, and afterward they have a clearer shape that can be transmitted, discussed, changed, and picked up again later. But expression is not free. Language asks you to choose words, an order, and boundaries, and many things that originally existed together have to be said one after another. Feelings that are mixed and hard to separate get cut into parts, and after being cut the thought is clearer and easier to transmit, but it is no longer the original thing. It has become a discrete version.

That discrete version is useful; without it thought cannot be taken out at all. But it also loses something. It keeps some main structure and turns the thought into a form that can be transmitted, saved, organized, and operated on, while the parts that did not enter language are temporarily compressed away. This is why I think compression is central here. Expression and recording do not simply move a thought out of the mind; they first compress it into a version that can be processed, and that version helps me preserve and organize the thought without being the whole original thing.

The harder part is not only that lan

title: Expression, Recording, and the Compressed Version of Thought (3)
body:
Expression, then, is not the transportation of thought; it is itself compression and rewriting. More precisely, it does not only rewrite the current thought, it shapes later thoughts, because the later self often continues from the expressed version. Different languages may work this way too, not in the simple sense that one makes people more rational, but in the sense that they cut experience differently. A continuous thought may naturally fall into certain blocks in one language and different blocks in another, and if the cut changes, the later path of thought may change with it. That may be why saying something in another language sometimes feels like it is no longer quite the same thing. It is not only a question of whether the translation is accurate; the original mass has been cut again.

This also makes me think about where AI fits. AI is very good at working with things that have already been expressed. Give it a paragraph, a pile of notes, a few claims, or scattered materials, and it can summarize, organize, classify, recombine, and find connections, making already-discretized material clearer and pointing out arrangements I may not have noticed. But what it receives is still the version I have already said. The original thought does not begin as text; it comes from experience, memory, emotion, the details of life, bodily state, unclear intuitions, and many connections that were never fully expressed. By the time I tell these things to AI, I have compressed them once already, so it is processing the material after that compression, not the mass that existed before language cut it apart.

So AI is useful to me, but its place is closer to an organizer and a reflecting surface than to the source of the thought. It can help me straighten out what has already been written, show me where things repeat, where they connect, and where they stay unclear, but I should not mistake that organization for thought itself. What I need to watch out for is continuing to work

title: Expression, Recording, and the Compressed Version of Thought (4)
body:
On the other hand, writing it down also fixes it into a certain version. This is not only a problem of being too early, though being too early does create problems, since a thought organized into a concept right after it appears can easily become narrower. Even if it is not too early, even after I have thought about it for a long time, once it is written it forms a version that will later influence me. I may keep thinking along its path, or be led by it, and that is what I need to be careful about. These notes matter because they mean I do not have to start from zero every time; they are like handles that let me hold onto things that would otherwise scatter. But the handle is not the thing itself, and if I trust the written versions too much they turn from handles into boundaries. I may begin to feel that a thought is exactly this, when it was only written this way at that moment.

So I do not want to treat these notes as definitions. They are more like slices from a certain day, recording where I had thought up to at that time without deciding where I can go later. Later I can change my mind, rewrite them, find that they connect elsewhere, or realize they were not that important after all. Titles, labels, and a little classification are useful, since without them things become messy and hard to find, but they should mainly be for retrieval, not for making boundaries. A thought that today seems to be about language may tomorrow connect to memory and the day after to consciousness, and it should not be stuck forever in one place just because it was first placed there. In the same way, a thought that has not been developed right now has not been excluded; it just has not grown there yet, and it may connect back later or not. Placing something too early on the irrelevant side is also a form of over-organization. I prefer to see these thoughts as a graph that slowly grows, each note a node with some lines around it, some clear now and some only faintly present, with mor

title: Rich Traces and Open Paths
body: AI can organize my thought better when it receives richer traces, but the most interesting cases are when rich traces still do not decide the path.

title: Rich Traces and Open Paths (2)
body:
I keep using voice input when I talk to AI. At first this looks like a convenience, since it is faster than typing and lets me keep talking before the thought disappears, but I think there is something deeper in it. A typed prompt is often already cleaned up. Before I type, I usually compress: I choose a direction, remove some hesitation, make the sentence more direct, and turn the thought into something that looks like a request. That can be useful, but it also means the AI receives a much thinner version of what was actually happening in my mind.

Voice is messier. I repeat myself, change direction halfway, say something and immediately feel it is not quite right, add examples, hesitate, reject my own wording, and suddenly remember another connection. The structure is not always good, but the movement is there, and that movement matters because it is also a trace. It is still not the original thought itself, which is an important distinction: the thought before expression may already have many layers, hidden pressure, bodily state, memory, attention, a vague conscious surface, and then the words that finally come out. By the time I speak, something has already been made explicit, and by the time the speech becomes text it has been changed again. So voice input is not direct access to the self, but it may be a richer externalized trace than a clean prompt.

It keeps more false starts, more local pressure, more signs of what I am resisting, and more evidence of which parts still feel unstable. When AI receives that kind of trace it has more to work with, and it can organize the material while preserving more of the path that produced it, so the structure it returns is less likely to feel alien. This also makes uptake easier. If I only give AI a short, polished request, its answer may be fluent but far from the actual problem, and I have to work hard to reconnect it to what I meant. If I give it a richer stream of my thinking, the structure it returns already contain

title: Rich Traces and Open Paths (3)
body:
The most interesting case is different. Sometimes I have already given a lot; the trace is rich, the examples are there, the failed formulations are there, the doubts and preferred directions are there, and still the path is not determined. That kind of uncertainty feels different from simply missing information. It may mean the problem itself is still open: the existing material is enough to rule out many bad continuations but not enough to decide the right one, and the uncertainty remains because the inquiry needs a new organizing judgment, not merely more completion. This is probably where research and creation often begin. In routine writing, enough context may make the answer converge, but in research writing even a rich context may leave several possible worlds open. The question is not only how to express the thought, but what the thought is becoming: which frame should hold it, which distinction should name it, which literature it should enter, which tension should stay unresolved for longer.

AI can help here, but in a different way. It should not simply close the space too quickly; its value may be to make the remaining uncertainty visible, to show several paths, organize why they differ, preserve what each one loses, and let me see where my own judgment still has to enter. So a good AI interaction is not always one that removes uncertainty. Sometimes it should increase resolution without closing the question. It can bring in a concept I did not know, an expert framing I could not have produced, or a contrast I had not seen, and that outside structure may genuinely change my thinking, but it becomes useful only if I can take it up: test it against my own pressure, resist it when it is too easy, and let it enter the next round of thought without letting it replace the inquiry.

So the practical lesson is not simply to give AI more information. More information helps when the problem is underconstrained because the trace is too thin, but when the trace is al

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People
body: Labels, goals, and rules make a complex person readable, expected, and constrained, then shape that person in return.

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People (2)
body:
If thoughts can be compressed by language, then people can be compressed by labels. A person is also a very complicated mass of things, with experiences, behaviors, habits, desires, fears, interests, abilities, and tendencies the person may not be able to explain clearly. Many of these are continuous; they do not naturally come in neat blocks. A person is not born already divided into personality, occupation, research direction, hobbies, values, and life stages. Very often those divisions are cut out later, so the person can be understood and communicated.

But we do need those cuts. Other people cannot directly read a person's full internal structure, since what you have done, what you have thought, why you react in certain ways, and why you care about some things and not others is too complicated to hand over each time. Most of the time you can only hand over a compressed version, and that is why labels are useful. A personality type is one kind, a professional identity another, a research direction another, a personal profile another, and so is the way other people describe what kind of person you are. We say we belong to a certain type, fit a certain direction, care about certain questions, or resemble a certain group, which makes us easier for others to read and easier for ourselves to organize.

A label is a bit like a language for describing people. It takes a continuous, complex, hard-to-transfer person and compresses them into a format that can travel more easily, so others quickly get a rough sense of who you are, what you care about, who you resemble, and how you differ, and you can also use it to gather scattered experiences, interests, behaviors, and thoughts into one place. This is not bad in itself. Without labels, people are hard to understand and hard to remember, and without some kind of identity narrative many experiences just stay scattered. A label works like a handle: it lets other people hold onto part of you, and it lets you hold onto part of

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People (3)
body:
More than that, a label can work like a training signal from the outside world. The outside cannot see a person's complete internal structure; it can only make compressed judgments from behavior, that this person is smart, outgoing, suited for research, has leadership, is unreliable, or belongs to a certain type. At first these may only be summaries based on some observed behavior, but once they appear they begin to change the environment. People treat you through those labels and give you different expectations, opportunities, feedback, and limitations. If you are seen as suited for something, you may receive more opportunities related to it and also be expected to keep performing in that direction; if you are seen as unsuited, you may be given fewer chances to try, and eventually you may stop trying yourself. So labels from the outside are not only descriptions; they change a person's training environment. They change how others respond to you and how you predict yourself, so you begin to know what others expect you to become and to adjust your behavior. Sometimes the adjustment is only on the surface, but over time the internal structure may also change, as habits, abilities, desires, and even your understanding of yourself shift.

A loop forms here. A person's internal structure produces behavior, the outside world compresses that behavior into labels, the labels become expectations, opportunities, rewards, and limits, and this feedback returns to the person and keeps shaping later behavior and internal structure. So a label is not a static sticker; it is part of a feedback system. This is not necessarily bad. A good label, or an accurate expectation, may push someone in a better direction: if someone says you are suited for something you may become more willing to invest in it and eventually grow that ability, and if a group recognizes some potential in you it may give you a path you would not have entered by yourself. But it can also be dangerous. A wrong labe

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People (4)
body:
Besides labels and goals, rules matter too. Sometimes a person's internal structure has not yet naturally produced the output society expects; their impulses, habits, desires, attention, or tendencies are not yet aligned with a certain social requirement. In that case society does not wait for the internal structure to slowly change but first uses rules to constrain behavior. Law, moral norms, politeness, institutions, school requirements, and family expectations are all rules of this kind, and they are also a form of discretization, compressing complex situations into explicit requirements: do not do this, say this instead, behave this way in this situation, suppress this impulse, this behavior will be punished. Rules are not quite the same as labels. Labels often say what you are or what others expect you to become, and goals say what you want to become, while rules are more like what you at least must not do, or what output you must produce.

Very often rules are not natural reactions at first. When a person sees something, they may first understand it through their existing internal structure, where past experience, habit, prejudice, desire, and fear shape what they notice, what they think of, and how they want to respond. That first reaction does not come from nowhere; it comes from internal representations that have already formed. But immediately after, another rule may intervene, one that is also in the mind but not fully internalized, coming from law, morality, politeness, education, or social expectation, telling you not to say this, not to do this, not to keep your attention there, not to let the first reaction become the final behavior. So the behavior that finally appears may not be the behavior the internal structure naturally generated; it may be the result after a rule-based override. The outside world sees compliant behavior, but the internal path may be completely different. One person may simply not have the impulse; another may have it first and

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People (5)
body:
This is a kind of internalization, where external discrete rules slowly become part of the internal structure and an originally strong tendency is worn down, weakened, or at least made easier to suppress. Whether a rule can be internalized, and how deeply, is itself a question. Not every rule truly changes internal structure; some remain surface compliance, some change habits, and some enter attention and judgment more deeply. And social rules are not always right. A rule is an external constraint mechanism, not truth itself; it can protect people but also limit them, help the internal structure become more stable but also press a person into an unreasonable shape. So I do not want to simply say labels are bad, goals are bad, or rules are bad. They are clearly useful. Without labels, others have a hard time reading you and you have a hard time organizing yourself; without goals, many behaviors do not hold together; without rules, society cannot rely only on everyone's naturally formed internal structure to maintain order. The question is not whether we should have these things, but whether we remember that none of them is neutral.

Labels make people readable, goals give people a point to move toward, and rules override output when the internal structure has not yet naturally aligned. All of them compress complex people into forms that are easier to handle, readable, expected, trainable, and constrained, and then they shape people in return through feedback, opportunities, rewards, punishments, repetition, and internalization. AI can be understood through this problem too, though here the issue is not whether it is the source of thought but that it can become a training signal. It does not only help me organize content; it also presents a way of moving thought forward, turning something vague into several points, the points into a structure, the structure into a next step, and the next step into an output that looks complete. This way of thinking is effective and ea

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People (6)
body: This is also why being summarized can feel subtly uncomfortable. It is not because a summary is always wrong; it is because a summary creates a version of you that is easier to circulate, one that may be smooth, clear, and convenient for others while failing to preserve the core that actually matters, so that later people may stop looking at you and start looking at the summarized version instead. Being misunderstood is bad, of course, but it is stranger when people seem to understand you while in fact they only understand a wrongly compressed version, and the clearer and more complete that version looks, the easier it is to believe it is you. That is stranger than simply being forgotten. Being forgotten is disappearance; being wrongly summarized leaves behind something that looks like you but whose inner structure may not be yours. So what interests me about labels, goals, and rules is not only whether they are accurate or right, but their two-way effect. They come from the person, or from society's demands on the person, and then they return to the person. They first make the person readable, expected, and constrained, and then they begin to change how the person continues becoming themselves. Labels can help us understand a person and help a person organize themselves, goals can give continuous behavior a direction, and rules can first override outputs that have not yet naturally aligned. But once they appear, they are no longer merely explanations or restrictions. They become expectation, feedback, training, constraint, and force. They compress a complex person into a version that society and the person can handle, and then they shape the person who has been compressed.

title: Memory Compresses Time, but the Self Is Changed by Passing Through It
body: Memory turns continuous life into stages and stories, but the self is shaped by actually moving through time.

title: Memory Compresses Time, but the Self Is Changed by Passing Through It (2)
body:
Life, while it is happening, does not really come in sections. It does not tell you that this part is high school, this part is university, this year is a turning point, this city means growth. Real time feels more like something that keeps flowing forward, and many changes do not have clear borders while they are happening. You simply live one day after another, slowly change, and only when you look back do you realize you seem to have entered another stage. But memory does not seem very good at preserving that continuity. The closer something is, the more detail it has: today still has temperature, something said yesterday still has a tone, a recent experience has not yet fully become a story. Once time moves further away, many things begin to be compressed; a year becomes a few keywords, a place becomes a feeling, a relationship becomes a few images, and many things that were once fine-grained end up as only 'that period.'

This compression does not happen evenly; it has a temporal gradient. The closer something is to the present, the lighter the compression and the looser the details, and the further away, the heavier the compression, with many things folded into stages, labels, stories, and a few representative scenes. Distant life is not really made only of a few keywords; it is just stored by memory at a higher compression rate. So sometimes I feel that so-called life stages may not be the structure of life itself, but a structure memory later adds to it. This is not necessarily bad. If the past remained there in full detail, maybe people could not keep moving forward. A stage is like a folder that gathers too many scattered things into one place, so you do not need to reopen every detail; you only need to say 'that time,' and many things come back with it.

But compression always loses something. You may remember a year as painful, when inside it there were probably ordinary afternoons, funny moments, and small joys that were not preserved; you may remember

title: Memory Compresses Time, but the Self Is Changed by Passing Through It (3)
body:
The further away time is, the more memory starts to look like labels. We need those labels, otherwise the past is too large to hold, but we also need to remember that the label is not the past itself. The past does not naturally come in chapters; we write it into chapters later so we can remember it. This also makes me think the self cannot be understood as something that exists at a single point in time. I do not really like saying the future self is another person. There is something to that saying, since the future version of me may think differently, like different things, care about different things, and even fail to understand why the present me cares so much about certain problems. But if I simply call the future self another person, something feels wrong, as if the continuous line in between had been cut.

A person is not a fixed point; a person is more like a trajectory that keeps changing. The version of me at each moment is not exactly the same. What I see today changes tomorrow's judgment, what happens tomorrow changes how I understand today the day after, and memory, body, relationships, and the internal representations in my mind all keep changing, so it is hard to say which slice is the real me. But these slices are not connected randomly; they change into one another little by little. The future me is not a stranger who suddenly appears in the distance; he is the state of this current system after many updates. Each step may be so tiny that it is hard to notice at the time, but many small changes together can become a clearly different person.

That is why the path in between matters. If you only look at two time points, a person can certainly seem very different; the me from ten years ago and the me now may look like two versions. But what makes both of them still me may not be how similar the versions are, but the continuous path between them. Even if another person knew my experiences and many of my memories, he could not directly become me, becau

title: Memory Compresses Time, but the Self Is Changed by Passing Through It (4)
body:
Thinking this way makes an old question I had even stranger. Suppose someone has just gone through a breakup and is in pain. Now there is a pill, and if he takes it, he can jump directly to the moment when the pain is over. It is not memory loss and it does not erase what happened; after the jump he still remembers everything and knows what he went through, feels he has lived through that period completely, and finds the memory and story continuous. The only thing skipped is the actual subjective experience of pain in the middle. What makes it stranger is that the person after the jump may not feel anything is missing. He may feel he really did go through it, and then the question becomes harder: if even the subjective feeling of continuity is preserved, what is still irreplaceable about actually experiencing it?

Should he take the pill? My first instinct is yes. It is simple: if the final state is the same and the memory is the same, why actually go through the pain? Pain itself is not something worth collecting, and if I can avoid it and keep the result, that sounds reasonable. But many people would not choose it. They would feel the painful period is also part of life, and that even if you remember it afterward, if you did not actually live through it, something is still missing. I have always found this strange. If the memory is truly complete, and the person after the jump believes he has lived through it, with the same lessons, memories, and result, then what exactly does actually living through it add?

Maybe memory and experience are still not the same thing. Memory is like the result, while experience is the process. Pain does not only leave a conclusion at the end; it changes a person second by second in the middle. Many things are not written into memory all at once; they slowly change you while you are enduring them. You may think receiving the same memory at the end is enough, but perhaps what really changes you is each morning you wake up, each moment

title: Memory Compresses Time, but the Self Is Changed by Passing Through It (5)
body: That is why I hesitate about the pill. If I take it, I lose the pain, but I may also lose a certain continuous change. The person after the jump keeps the compressed memory and the feeling of having come through, but he has not actually walked through that time; the memory is there and the result is there, but the process is not. Maybe remembering a road and actually walking it are not the same thing. And yet I cannot say I definitely would not take it; if pain could really be skipped, I might still want to skip it. I just understand better now why some people would not. The self is not a photograph, and not just a compressed memoir; it is more like a movement, where no moment is the final version but each moment grows out of the one before it. Memory compresses time into stages, making the past something that can be preserved, narrated, and understood again, but what truly changes a person may not only be those stages and stories. It may also be the continuous passage through time that was never fully preserved. What we remember later are chapters; what we lived then was a whole line.

title: Satisfaction Is Not a Fixed Reward
body: Satisfaction is better understood as a changing signal generated through behavior, time, individual difference, and interpretation.

title: Satisfaction Is Not a Fixed Reward (2)
body:
I sometimes wonder whether happiness and satisfaction can be treated as the same thing. This is obviously debatable. Some people would say happiness is more than satisfaction, that it also includes meaning, long-term goals, relationships, a sense of value, and even growth through pain. But I tend to first understand happiness as a kind of satisfaction, and the important point is that satisfaction cannot be defined too narrowly. It cannot just mean whether something feels good right now. Understood more broadly, it can include many things: eating something good in the moment is a kind of satisfaction, but so are delayed gratification, finishing a goal, controlling an impulse, helping someone, getting a good deal, and even feeling that I made the more rational choice.

So delayed gratification is not necessarily the opposite of satisfaction; it may simply be another form of it. Some people treat immediate gratification as lower and delayed gratification as higher, and I think that is too simple. A person who chooses delayed gratification may not be rejecting satisfaction but getting it from the delay itself, from the future payoff, from self-control, or from the feeling of having made the better choice. They are not avoiding satisfaction; they are choosing another satisfaction curve. What is called more rational may not be separate from satisfaction either. In that person's system the choice is expected to produce a stronger, more stable, or longer-lasting signal, so rationality and satisfaction do not have to be opposites; sometimes rationality itself is a source of satisfaction.

I do not need to reduce satisfaction to one specific biological measure, but I can at least think of it as an internal feedback signal. Different activities trigger different signals, and different people receive them with different strengths, durations, delays, and shapes. Eating, shopping, studying, earning money, volunteering, being recognized, completing a plan, controlling desire, all

title: Satisfaction Is Not a Fixed Reward (3)
body:
Take buying discounted products. Some people really enjoy getting discounts. They spend time comparing, searching, and waiting for deals, and finally buy something at a good price. Someone else may think this is a waste of time, that you save a little money but spend a lot of energy, so why not just buy it directly and use the time for something else.
