From the perspective of opportunity cost, that argument makes sense, but it misses something: the act of finding a discount may itself produce satisfaction. For some people getting a discount is not only about saving money; it feels like a small win, with the search, the comparison, the surprise of finding a good deal, the sense of being good at living, and the feeling that resources are under better control. It can even feel like a little game: I found it, I judged correctly, I won a little. If the process itself gives that person satisfaction, then it is not simply a waste of time.

For another person the same process may produce no joy at all, only trouble, exhaustion, anxiety, and wasted attention, and for them buying at the original price may be more rational, because what they save is not a little money but time, energy, and mental space. So the same behavior can be rational in different ways for different people, not because rationality is arbitrary, but because the behavior produces different satisfaction signals once it enters different internal systems. From the outside both people are spending time looking for discounts, but internally the returns are different, so the rational calculation is different too. You cannot judge whether something is worth it only by asking how much time was spent and how much money was saved; you also have to ask what the person received from the process. Opportunity cost should not be calculated only in money and time. More accurately, it is the satisfaction you give up from another possible activity. If someone spends an hour looking for discounts and enjoys the process, that h

title: Satisfaction Is Not a Fixed Reward (4)
body:
This makes me think satisfaction is not something the outside world simply hands to a person. It is not that one behavior is naturally joyful and another naturally boring; often the behavior itself is only the material, and the actual satisfaction is generated through a person's internal structure, interpretation, goal system, and the unfolding of time. This connects to what I have been thinking about labels and language. If the same activity is interpreted as 'wasting time,' the experience may be irritation; if it is interpreted as 'I completed a small optimization,' the experience may be satisfaction. A person who sees discount-hunting as poverty or trouble is not experiencing the same event as someone who sees it as a game, a strategy, or a life skill. This does not mean reality changes just because we describe it differently, and it does not mean all pain can be solved by reinterpretation, which would be too shallow. But interpretation does take part in shaping experience. The satisfaction produced by a behavior does not come only from the behavior itself; it also comes from how the person understands it, and that understanding is connected to their goals, identity, experience, and habits, so the same activity can become a completely different psychological event in different people.

This is why I think people may be able to create satisfaction to some extent. By creation I do not mean forcing oneself to be happy or rationalizing everything bad; creating satisfaction is not pretending a bad thing is good. It is redesigning the relationship between a behavior and oneself so the behavior can genuinely produce some positive signal. People can use attention, interpretation, habit design, and goal setting to make some originally neutral activities capable of producing satisfaction. Take housework. For some people it is just a burden, but if someone understands it as restoring order, organizing space, and making life feel controllable again, it may begin to produce s

title: Satisfaction Is Not a Fixed Reward (5)
body:
So satisfaction is not only about quantity; it is also about how satisfaction is generated. Some comes from the result, some from approaching the result, some from waiting, some from control, some from interpretation, and some from the feeling that one is becoming a certain kind of person. That is why delayed gratification and immediate gratification should not be treated as simple opposites. Immediate gratification is one signal and delayed gratification is another; they simply have different shapes in time. A person may be willing to wait because the result after waiting is larger, but also because waiting itself is connected to self-control, planning, and a sense of the future, which produces another kind of satisfaction. And something as small as buying discounted products is not merely a consumption issue; it shows something larger, that people do not receive happiness directly from the objective world but generate it inside their own interpretive systems.

This also changes how I think about social progress. Social development makes many things easier to obtain, and that is good; reducing disease, poverty, violence, and helplessness is clearly progress. But if satisfaction is not simply determined by external resources, then more resources do not necessarily translate linearly into more happiness. A person is not a container; you do not simply pour more good things in and automatically get more happiness out. The satisfaction system adapts, compares, recalibrates, and generates different signals under different interpretations. So a person with better living conditions is not always more satisfied, and a person with fewer resources is not completely unable to generate satisfaction. The former may become saturated because things arrive too quickly, while the latter may still find structure, expectation, and process in small things. Of course this should not be used to cover up real problems. Poverty, illness, oppression, and long-term stress cannot be solved by

title: Oysters and Black Truffle Toast at Queen Victoria Market
body: A Melbourne food memory from my last day before leaving exchange: oysters with lemon juice and black truffle toast at Queen Victoria Market.

title: Oysters and Black Truffle Toast at Queen Victoria Market (2)
body:
Queen Victoria Market in Melbourne gave me one of those food memories that stayed with me after I left the city. The oysters were fresh, cold, and clean, and a squeeze of lemon made the taste even sharper and clearer. The black truffle toast was unexpectedly good too: warm, rich, fragrant, and straightforward.

What fixes it in my memory is that I went with my sister on my last day of the Melbourne exchange. It was as much about the timing as the food, a final market morning and a final meal before leaving, something simple that ended up tied to the whole exchange. It is one of the things I still miss about Melbourne.

title: Chaoshan Beef Hot Pot in Guangzhou
body: A Guangzhou food memory with my father: Chaoshan beef hot pot, especially beef balls and diaolong, eaten without sauce for the clean beef flavor.

title: Chaoshan Beef Hot Pot in Guangzhou (2)
body:
When I travelled to Guangzhou with my father, we went for Chaoshan beef hot pot together. That is part of why the meal stuck with me, not only because the food was good, but because it became part of that trip with him.

I really like Chaoshan-style beef, especially the beef balls and the diaolong. The beef balls have that springy texture and deep beefy taste, and diaolong is clean and tender in a way that works well in hot pot. I also tend not to dip it in any sauce; the plain flavor of the beef is good enough on its own, and I would rather not cover it up.

title: AI-Generated Kitten Videos and Manufactured Cuteness
body: AI-generated kitten videos are a good example of synthetic content still reaching human senses and triggering real emotional responses.

title: AI-Generated Kitten Videos and Manufactured Cuteness (2)
body:
I have seen a lot of AI-generated videos on social media where kittens interact with people, along with more surreal ones, like a plush watermelon being opened to reveal little kittens inside. I find them very cute, and clearly plenty of other people do too.

What interests me is not just that they are cute, but that they show generative content triggering real emotion. The kittens are not real in the usual sense, yet the videos still reach human perception through softness, smallness, eye contact, movement, care, and surprise, and those signals are enough to move people.

That is part of what makes this kind of content valuable: it can create things that touch the senses even when the objects are synthetic. It also calls for some caution, because the same ability can be abused. If generated content can reliably press emotional buttons, it can also be used to manipulate attention, sympathy, desire, or belief. So the value of generative content is not only about how realistic it is; often it lies in producing emotionally meaningful sensory patterns, and precisely because those patterns work on people, they need to be handled with care.

title: A Conversation About Identity
body: After taking part in an interview about growing up between Shanghai and Taiwanese family roots, I found a clearer way to describe identity: not confused, but layered.

title: A Conversation About Identity (2)
body:
I once took part in an interview about identity and about growing up with a Taiwanese family background while living mostly in Shanghai. I had not planned to write about it publicly, but the conversation helped me organize a lot of scattered feelings that had sat in the background for a long time.

What stayed with me was not a dramatic conclusion but a quieter one: identity does not have to be a single answer. It can be made of several layers at once, where I grew up, where my family comes from, the languages and habits that shaped me, the places that feel familiar, and the way I introduce myself in different rooms.

The interview helped me see that this is not really confusion so much as a layered structure, where different parts become more visible in different situations. Sometimes Shanghai is the most natural answer; sometimes the Taiwanese family roots matter more, and neither layer cancels the other. That is why the conversation meant something to me: it gave me a calmer way to look back at my background without forcing it into one simple label.

title: Napier Seaside with My Mother
body: My mother used to drive me from Hastings or Havelock North to Napier, and somehow that seaside became attached to her.

title: Napier Seaside with My Mother (2)
body:
Napier seaside is one of the places I connect with my mother. She lives around Hastings and Havelock North, and when I went back to New Zealand she would sometimes drive me to Napier. It was not a big trip, just a short drive to the water.

I still remember it quite clearly: the car ride, the road, arriving near the sea, walking for a while, maybe not even talking much. It was ordinary, but it was one of the few things we did together there again and again.

My family's connection with New Zealand is a little scattered. My sister studied there, my mother stayed for her, and later that area became part of my mother's own life too. For me the country was never only a travel destination; it was also where part of my family was living without me. So when she drove me to Napier, it felt like I was briefly stepping into that part of her life.

I don't remember Napier as just a beautiful seaside so much as somewhere my mother brought me, and maybe that is enough.

title: My Mother’s Cooking Across Places
body: A memory of my mother’s cooking, from home in Shanghai to later visits in New Zealand, and how food can carry the feeling of home across places.

title: My Mother’s Cooking Across Places (2)
body:
My mother has always been good at making food that is both healthy and genuinely good. When we were still living together in Shanghai, this felt completely normal. She cooked at home and I ate what she made, and at the time I did not think of it as anything worth remembering. It was just part of home.

Later, after life became more scattered and she spent more time in New Zealand, her cooking started to feel different. Visiting her there, a meal she had made was no longer just a normal meal; it became a small return to something familiar. The food itself matters, of course, usually clean, warm, and carefully made, but what I remember more is the continuity. The same person who cooked for us in Shanghai was now cooking in another country, at another stage of life, and the taste still carried something from before.

That is what makes it feel like home. Home is not fixed in one city or one apartment or one period of life; sometimes it shows up through a repeated act, someone still cooking for you and still caring whether you have eaten well. I do not get to eat my mother's cooking often now, and maybe that is why I notice it more when I do.

title: Trying Golf with My Mother in New Zealand
body: A small New Zealand memory: my mother liked golf and brought me along a few times, even though I never felt very natural at it.

title: Trying Golf with My Mother in New Zealand (2)
body:
When I visited my mother in New Zealand, she sometimes brought me along to play golf. It is one of her hobbies, and I could tell she enjoyed the whole rhythm of it: the open grass, the slow walking, choosing a club, taking her time before a shot. It suited her.

I tried a few times, but I never felt I had much talent for it. I could hit the ball, just not the way good players do, where it lifts cleanly and draws a nice curve through the air; mine was usually more practical than pretty, and sometimes not even practical. Still, I liked going with her, not because I had suddenly taken to golf, but because it was something she had made part of her life there, and for a few afternoons I got to be inside it too.

title: Racket Games with the SLR Lab
body: A small SLR Lab memory: our PI took me and another RA to play badminton and table tennis, and the lab felt a little warmer because of it.

title: Racket Games with the SLR Lab (2)
body:
One time the PI of our SLR Lab took me and another RA out to play badminton and table tennis. It was not a serious sports day. We played, missed shots, picked up balls, laughed a bit, and drifted between the two games in the slightly messy way small group outings usually go.

I liked it because it made the lab feel like more than papers, meetings, and tasks. Those things matter, but a lab also has a feeling to it, and you can tell whether people are just working in the same place or whether there is some actual warmth around the work. That afternoon tipped it towards the second, nothing dramatic, just a good lab atmosphere showing up on an ordinary day.

title: The Sea Near My First Hong Kong Life
body: A memory of Kennedy Town seaside from my earlier HKU years, when the sea was close enough to become part of ordinary student life.

title: The Sea Near My First Hong Kong Life (2)
body:
I have not been to Kennedy Town seaside much lately, which feels a little strange, because in my first years at HKU, when I still lived closer to that side, the sea felt much more reachable. It was not a destination, just somewhere nearby I could go without making it a plan.

I do not remember every walk clearly now; a lot of it has probably become implicit. But I think the place still affected me. Being near the water changed the feeling of the city. Hong Kong can feel dense and fast and pressed together, and then I would walk toward the sea and the space would suddenly open. Part of that feeling may have come from New Zealand too, from the times my mother took me to the seaside there, so the sea was already tied to something familiar before I arrived in Hong Kong. Kennedy Town was not only a new place; it also touched an older feeling.

What I liked was not a dramatic view but the movement of the water, the irregular waves, the space beyond the buildings, and the simple fact that the city could suddenly end in something wide. Looking back, that seaside belongs to a particular stage of my Hong Kong life. I do not live that rhythm now and I do not go there as often, but when I think of my early HKU years, the sea is still somewhere in the background.

title: Ferry Rides Across Sydney Harbour
body: A Sydney memory of taking public ferries across the harbour, moving between the Opera House, the bridge, quiet residential areas, commuters, and sunset.

title: Ferry Rides Across Sydney Harbour (2)
body:
One of my strongest memories of Sydney is not standing at a single landmark but moving through the harbour by ferry. The ferries were cheap, so we kept taking them between different stops, and that made the city feel less like a fixed map and more like something you could cross through from the water, each route showing a slightly different version of it.

Sometimes the ferry passed near the Opera House and the Harbour Bridge, and everything looked exactly as beautiful as people say. Other parts were quieter, more residential, with houses near the water that looked calm and expensive and almost unreal from the deck. I remember one ride around the time people were finishing work, with commuters in suits heading home; when the ferry reached their stop they got off in an orderly line and walked toward those neighborhoods, and for a moment I felt like I was watching someone else's ordinary day from the outside, work in the city, a ferry across the water, home somewhere along the harbour.

The sunset made all of it better, the bridge and the Opera House and the water and the light coming together in a way that was almost too easy to remember. Sydney stayed with me because of that movement, not one view but the feeling of crossing between views, letting the city change slowly around the boat.

title: Gold Coast, Water, and the City Line
body: A Gold Coast memory of water activities, high views, and the strange beauty of a city stretched directly along the beach.

title: Gold Coast, Water, and the City Line (2)
body:
Gold Coast is one of the places where I remember the sea not just as something to look at but as something I actually got into. We did a lot of water activities there: kayaking, jet skiing, snorkelling, and the one where a boat pulls you up into the air above the water. I am not even sure what I called it at the time, but I remember the feeling clearly, the sea turning from background into part of the body, the speed, the wind, and the noise.

Another memory is going up one of those high observation decks. From above, the whole city looked like a line pressed against the coast, beach and water and towers and long strips of sunlight all arranged together. It felt different from other cities I had seen. In a lot of places the sea and the city feel separate, but in Gold Coast the buildings seemed to stand right beside the beach, almost as if the city had been drawn along the edge of the water.

I liked that contrast a lot, both urban and coastal, both energetic and holiday-like; you could be in a high-rise and still feel that everything was organized by the beach. When I think of Gold Coast now, I think of water from two directions, being on it and later looking down at it from high above.

title: A Road Trip Along the Great Ocean Road
body: A Melbourne exchange memory of friends renting a car and driving along the Great Ocean Road, where the journey mattered as much as the views.

title: A Road Trip Along the Great Ocean Road (2)
body:
The Great Ocean Road is hard to write about, because the first thing to say is too obvious: it was beautiful. What I remember more is that this was during my Melbourne exchange, and some friends rented a car and brought us along the road. Without them it would probably have stayed one of those places I knew I should visit; because of them it became part of my actual life there.

That day was not only about seeing the coastline. It was hours in the car with people who, a few months earlier, I had not even known, stopping wherever someone thought the view was worth it, taking photos, getting back in, talking, getting tired, and still wanting to see the next stop. Exchange friendships are strange that way. They form quickly, but they can carry a lot, because everyone is living inside a temporary period and knows it will not last in the same form, so the things you do together end up heavier than they look.

The road kept changing, cliffs and ocean and open sky, small stops and famous viewpoints and places I later put on my website map, but I do not remember it as one clean scene. I remember it as movement, the car, the people, and the sense that Melbourne was becoming more than a university term. For me the Great Ocean Road is not only a scenic route; it is one of the moments when the exchange started to feel like a real lived period, because there were people beside me who made it feel that way.

title: A Birthday Somewhere Along Taiwan’s East Coast
body: A family road trip along Taiwan’s east coast, with my father driving, seaside cafés along the road, and one of my birthdays folded into the journey.

title: A Birthday Somewhere Along Taiwan’s East Coast (2)
body:
One of my happiest recent family memories is a road trip along Taiwan's east coast. It was the four of us, my father, my mother, my sister, and me. My father drove, and we made our way down the eastern side of the island, stopping in different places along the way. I do not remember every exact turn now, and maybe that is fine, because what stayed with me is not the route as a record but the feeling of being together in one car.

That kind of full family time has become rare. Everyone is in different places now, with different lives and schedules, so when we are all together for a trip, even ordinary things start to feel memorable. The east coast had a lot of sea. We would drive, catch the water, stop somewhere, and sometimes find one of those small cafés near the coast, each with its own style, and sit down with a coffee, look out at the sea, and then keep going.

There was nothing dramatic about those stops, and that is exactly why I liked them. They were not arranged to become important memories; they became important because they were simple, repeated, and shared. My birthday fell somewhere inside the trip too, which made the whole thing feel warmer, not a formal celebration but a birthday folded into a moving family journey, somewhere between the sea, the road, and coffee by the coast. When I think back to it, I do not picture a single landmark first. I remember my father driving, the four of us together, and Taiwan's east coast passing by outside the window.

title: Athens, Ruins, and a Rare Family Trip
body: A family trip to Greece, where food, ruins, sunlight, and ancient stone made the place feel unlike anywhere I had been before.

title: Athens, Ruins, and a Rare Family Trip (2)
body:
Our family trip to Greece stayed with me partly because it was another rare stretch when the four of us could be together for a longer time. We stayed about two weeks, mostly around Athens, visiting different places, eating a lot, and moving through a city that felt very different from the ones I was used to. Greece was not only pretty; it had a historical weight I had not really felt before.

The ruins were the clearest part of that. Places like the Acropolis made me feel I was not just looking at an old building but standing near something that belonged to a much longer line of human history, the stone and the columns and the open air and the sheer age of it all making the place feel almost unreal. There was one part my mother especially liked, with carved figures standing in place of columns; I cannot always call up the name, but I remember seeing them there, exposed to the sky, still holding that shape after so much time.

And then there was the food. I really liked Greek food, the Greek salad, the fried calamari, the halloumi, and a lot of other things that were simple but very satisfying, and it kept the trip feeling relaxed rather than only grand or historical. That combination is what I remember most: ancient places in the daytime, good food after walking, and my family there together. Greece felt like a place where travel was not only about seeing something new but about feeling that the world is much older and wider than my usual life.

title: Swimming in the Mediterranean
body: A Greece memory of taking a boat out to sea, swimming in the Mediterranean, and feeling the trip become physical rather than only visual.

title: Swimming in the Mediterranean (2)
body:
One of my clearest memories from Greece is swimming in the Mediterranean. We went out by boat that day, and I remember the sun, the water, the open sea around us, and my family nearby. There was not much plot to it, but it stayed with me clearly.

The strange part is that the Mediterranean had always been just a name to me before then, a sea on maps and in history and in photos of Greece, and then suddenly I was in it rather than looking at it from the shore. I think it was my only time swimming there, which makes the memory feel more fixed. There are plenty of beautiful views from that trip, but this one feels different because it was physical; the place was not only something I saw, it touched my body.

We spent time on the boat, rested in the sun, swam, and let the day pass slowly, and nothing needed to be explained. When I think of Greece I do not only think of ruins and streets and blue views from far away. I also think of that day on the water, and the feeling of entering a sea I had known before only as a name.

title: Rainy Days as Permission to Stay In
body: A small daily feeling: rain makes staying indoors feel less like avoidance and more like something the world has quietly allowed.

title: Rainy Days as Permission to Stay In (2)
body:
I do not actually like rainy days that much. Rain is inconvenient: the ground gets wet, the sky turns grey, and going anywhere becomes a little more annoying. But there is one thing I do like about it, which is that it gives me a reason not to go out.

On a sunny day, staying inside can feel like wasting the day. Even if nobody says anything, there is a small pressure to do something, meet someone, walk somewhere, make proper use of the weather. Rain takes that pressure away. It makes staying in feel less like a personal choice and more like the natural thing to do, and the room becomes enough, not because I suddenly love being cooped up but because the outside has stopped demanding anything. So maybe it is not the rain I like, but the excuse it gives me.

title: The Mental Map of a Small Room
body: A small-room memory about mess, order, and why a space can feel familiar even when it does not look perfectly tidy from the outside.

title: The Mental Map of a Small Room (2)
body:
My mother sometimes says my room is messy, and she is probably not entirely wrong. From the outside it can look like things are scattered everywhere. But to me the room is not simply messy, because I usually know where everything is; there is a map of it in my head.

That map is not visible to anyone else. A book on one side, a cable somewhere else, a camera lens near the window, some small thing on the desk. It may not look organized in the usual sense, but I know the routes, and I know where my hand should go when I need something. That is why small rooms can feel intimate to me. In a small space every object is close enough to become part of the mental map; the room may be crowded, but it is navigable, with a kind of personal order that only fully makes sense from inside my own habits.

A larger space is more comfortable in an obvious way, but it can also feel less close, the things more spread out and the relationship between me and the objects around me less clear. So when I say my room is not really messy, I do not mean it is objectively tidy. I mean it has a structure I can feel, a little private, a little inefficient, maybe not very presentable, but still mine.

title: My First Camera and Another Way of Seeing
body: A memory of my Nikon Zfc, the apartment window, long lenses, travel photos, and how a camera gave me another way to look at the world.

title: My First Camera and Another Way of Seeing (2)
body:
My Nikon Zfc was my first real camera. At the start I did not even need to go far; I would stand in the apartment and shoot through the small window, trying different angles, looking for things outside that I would not normally notice. After I got a longer lens it became even more interesting, since the lens could pull distant things closer and turn a random view out the window into something worth looking at.

That was probably when photography began to change my relationship with going out. The camera gave me a reason to walk more, to look around more, and later to travel with a slightly different kind of attention, not only going somewhere but also looking for frames. My mother has said that people should use their eyes to see the world instead of always taking photos, and I think she is right. There are things the eyes take in directly that a photo cannot replace, the scale of a place, the air, the movement, the feeling of being there.

But I think the camera offers another kind of seeing rather than a copy of the eyes. It cuts the world differently, notices details I might have passed over, and lets me return to a moment later and see it again from a distance. Sometimes a photo is valuable not because it is beautiful but because it proves I once looked at the world from that exact angle. That is what the camera gave me, not a replacement for experience but another layer of it.

title: Music That Became More Spacious
body: A small reflection on how my music taste moved from stronger beats and rap-like rhythm toward slower, wider, more atmospheric sound.

title: Music That Became More Spacious (2)
body:
My music taste has changed quite a bit. In high school I was more drawn to music with strong rhythm, EDM and rap and beats that moved quickly and gave a direct kind of energy. I still understand why I liked it; that kind of music pushes you forward, and it is useful when you want intensity or speed or just a clear hit of momentum.

These days I find myself drawn to music that feels more spacious. Not music without rhythm, since rhythm still matters, but something slower, wider, and more atmospheric. Sometimes it is purely instrumental, and sometimes it still has lyrics, like Lana Del Rey's Young and Beautiful, where what I like is not only the words but the whole feeling behind them, the slow movement, the large background, the sense that the song opens up a space rather than just driving a beat.

I do not think this means my old taste was wrong. Different stages of life just seem to call for different sound structures. EDM still works for exercise, because the body wants energy and repetition, but when I am not exercising I usually want music that leaves me more room to think or feel. Maybe that is the change: I used to want music to move me forward, and now I also want it to give me space.
